title: Legislative Changes Regarding the Conduct of U.S. Trade Remedy Investigations
bodyHtml:
In a letter received from the Ministry of Commerce, it is stated that on May 9, 2023, the International Trade Administration (ITA) of the U.S. Department of Commerce submitted a series of proposals to improve, strengthen, and enhance the enforcement of anti-dumping (AD) and countervailing duty (CVD) laws by amending 19 CFR Part 351, for the consideration of relevant parties. The draft presented in May 2023, including some amendments, has been published in the U.S. Federal Register dated March 25, 2024 

https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/03/25/2024-05509/regulations-improving-and-strengthening-the-enforcement-of-trade-remedies-through-the-administration

and it is noted that these changes will come into effect as of April 24, 2024.

Furthermore, the letter highlights some key points regarding the mentioned changes:

For the first time in production cost calculations, ITA may take into account whether labor rights, environment, property, or intellectual property rights are being protected, the existence of laws to protect them, and their protectionist nature. For example, opinions regarding lower production costs for a foreign producer not required to comply with workplace safety laws may be reflected in the calculations. Additionally, penalties not collected from firms violating environmental/labor rights or intellectual property rights may be considered as financial contributions provided by the government within subsidy investigations.

Another debated change is the removal of the provision on transnational subsidies. Previously, under this provision, subsidies provided to producers in the investigated country by a third country were not considered countervailable, but the removal of this provision opens the way for third-country subsidies to be considered countervailable. Similar to the margin calculation applied to Indonesia in the ongoing cold-rolled steel investigation by the EU, it will be possible to include subsidies provided by third countries like China through initiatives such as the "Belt and Road" Initiative in margin calculations.

Changes have been made regarding considerations in cases of existence of distortions due to significant market conditions (PMS). Subsidies provided through debt forgiveness will be considered non-recurring subsidies.

New timing and quotation requirements for parties involved in providing recorded information have been introduced, and it is considered important for the creation of relevant questionnaire responses to use ACCESS barcodes when referring to previous decisions made by ITA and other such changes.
